title: Strings & StringBuilder
description: Master Java String immutability, the full method toolkit, and StringBuilder's mutable API — the concepts that generate the most output-prediction and error-identification questions in CSE110 exams.

sections.content: 📝 Why Strings Are Exam Favourites

CSE110 exams love String questions because they test three concepts simultaneously: **immutability** (does the original change?), **reference vs value comparison** (`==` vs `.equals()`), and **method chaining** (what does a sequence of calls actually return?). A solid mental model of how Strings work in the JVM saves you from the most common point-losing mistakes.
🧊 String Immutability

In Java, `String` objects are **immutable** — once created, their character sequence can never change. Every method that appears to modify a String (like `toUpperCase()`, `replace()`, `trim()`) actually **returns a new String** object and leaves the original unchanged. If you don't capture the return value, the modification is silently lost.

sections.title: Immutability — Modifications Return New Strings

sections.label: 🚨 #1 Exam Mistake: Ignoring the Return Value
sections.content: Calling `s.replace('a', 'b')` or `s.toUpperCase()` **does nothing** unless you capture the return value. In predict-output questions, always check whether the result of a String method is **reassigned** or **used directly**. If it's called but not used, the String is unchanged.
🔗 String Pool and the == vs .equals() Trap

String literals (`"hello"`) are stored in the **String Pool** (a special area of the heap). Two literals with the same content share the same object, so `==` returns `true`. But Strings created with `new String("hello")` always create a fresh heap object, so `==` returns `false` even if the content is identical. Always use `.equals()` for content comparison.

sections.title: String Pool: == vs .equals()

sections.label: Compile-Time vs Runtime Concatenation
sections.content: When both operands of `+` are **compile-time constants** (literals or `final` variables), the result is interned into the pool. When at least one operand is a **variable** (non-final), the result is a new heap object. This is why `"ja" + "va" == "java"` is `true` but `x + "va" == "java"` is `false` when `x` is a non-final variable.
🛠️ Essential String Methods Reference

rows: First index of substring (-1 if absent)
True if empty or only whitespace (Java 11+)

sections.content: 🔢 String Index Rules

Indices are **zero-based**: the first character is at index 0, the last at `length()-1`. `substring(i, j)` includes index `i` but **excludes** index `j` — so the result has `j - i` characters. Accessing an invalid index throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` at **runtime**.

sections.title: substring() Indexing — Common Exam Trace

sections.content: 🏗️ StringBuilder — Mutable String Building

`StringBuilder` is a **mutable** sequence of characters. Unlike `String`, its methods modify the object **in place** — there is no discarded return value problem. It is preferred over String concatenation inside loops because repeated `+` on Strings creates O(n) new objects; `StringBuilder.append()` is O(1) amortized. `StringBuffer` is the thread-safe (synchronized) variant — in single-threaded exam code, always use `StringBuilder`.

sections.title: StringBuilder API Showcase

sections.content: 📊 StringBuilder Method Reference

rows: Appends x (any type) to end; modifies in place
Inserts x at position i; shifts rest right
Removes characters from i (incl) to j (excl)
Removes single char at index i
Replaces indices i..j-1 with String s
Reverses the entire sequence in place
Returns char at index i (same as String)
Sets char at index i to c
First index of substring s (-1 if absent)

sections.label: StringBuilder Methods Return `this`
sections.content: Most StringBuilder methods return the **same** StringBuilder object (`this`), enabling method chaining: `sb.append("a").append("b").reverse()`. The chained result is still the **same** object as `sb`. This is the opposite of String, where every method returns a **new** object.
⚡ String vs StringBuilder Performance

Concatenating `n` strings in a loop using `+` creates O(n) intermediate String objects and copies O(n²) total characters — extremely wasteful. `StringBuilder.append()` in a loop is O(n) total. This difference is tested conceptually in exams as a design question.

sections.title: String Loop vs StringBuilder Loop

sections.content: 🔄 String ↔ Numeric Conversions
🏋️ Practice Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Immutability — What Does Each Line Print?
sections.answer:
Hello World
Hello World
Hi World
HELLO
sections.explanation:
Line by line:
1. `s.replace("World","Java")` — creates a new String "Hello Java" but the result is **discarded**. `s` still references "Hello World".
   → prints: `Hello World`
2. `t = s.replace("Hello","Hi")` — creates new String "Hi World" and assigns it to `t`. `s` is still unchanged.
   → `System.out.println(s)` prints: `Hello World`
   → `System.out.println(t)` prints: `Hi World`
3. `s.toUpperCase()` creates `"HELLO WORLD"`, then `.substring(0,5)` extracts `"HELLO"` (indices 0–4). This new String is reassigned to `s`.
   → prints: `HELLO`

sections.title: Exercise 2: StringBuilder Chaining and Mutation
sections.explanation:
Initial: `"abcdef"` (length 6)
Step 1: `delete(2,4)` removes indices 2 (inclusive) to 4 (exclusive) — removes chars 'c','d'. Result: `"abef"` (length 4).
Step 2: `insert(2, "XY")` inserts at position 2. Characters at index 2+ shift right. Result: `"abXYef"` (length 6).
Step 3: `reverse()` reverses in place. `"abXYef"` reversed character by character is `"feYXba"`. Note: 'X'→'X', 'Y'→'Y', case preserved.
Length of `"feYXba"` = 6.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Bug in String Comparison and Mutation
sections.error: Line 6: `input == password` compares **references**, not content — this is a logical error. Because `input` was created with `new String(...)`, it is a different heap object from the literal `"Secret123"` even though they contain the same characters. The condition evaluates to `false` and prints "Access denied" instead of "Access granted". Line 11: `password.toLowerCase()` result is discarded — logical error. `password` remains `"Secret123"`.
sections.explanation: Two separate bugs: (1) `==` on Strings compares object identity, not content. `new String("Secret123")` always allocates a fresh object on the heap, so its reference can never be `==` to the literal's pool reference. The fix is `.equals()`, which compares the actual character sequences. (2) `String.toLowerCase()` (like all String methods) returns a NEW String and leaves the original unchanged. Without reassigning `password = password.toLowerCase()`, the method call has no observable effect. Both are **logical errors** — the code compiles and runs, just produces the wrong output.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Method Chaining and String Arithmetic
sections.explanation:
s = "  Java Programming  " (20 chars including spaces)

1. s.trim() → "Java Programming" (16 chars). .length() → 16.

2. s.trim() → "Java Programming", .substring(5) → "Programming" (indices 5 to end). .toUpperCase() → "PROGRAMMING".

3. s.trim() → "Java Programming". First .replace("a","@") → "J@v@ Progr@mming". Then .replace("@","A") → "JAvA ProgrAmming". The second replace only targets @, not original a characters (already replaced in step one).

4. s.indexOf("a") searches original s (with leading spaces). Characters: index 0=' ', 1=' ', 2='J', 3='a'. First 'a' is at index 3.

5. s.trim().indexOf("a"): trimmed string is "Java Programming". Characters: 0='J', 1='a'. First 'a' is at index 1.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Reverse Words in a Sentence
sections.approach:
1. Split the string by spaces using String.split(" ") to get an array of words.
2. Iterate the array in reverse order, appending each word to a StringBuilder.
3. Add a space between words (but not after the last one).
4. Return sb.toString().
sections.explanation: split(" ") breaks the sentence into an array at each space. Iterating backwards from words.length-1 to 0 appends words in reverse order. The `if (i > 0)` guard adds a space between consecutive words but skips the trailing space after the last word. StringBuilder is preferred over String concatenation in the loop to avoid O(n²) copies. Edge case: single-word input — the loop runs once, `i > 0` is false, so no space is appended. Result is just that word.

sections.content: 📋 Quick Reference Summary

items: **String is immutable**: all methods return a NEW String; original is unchanged unless you reassign (`s = s.toUpperCase()`)
**== vs .equals()**: use `==` for reference identity (pool/object), `.equals()` for content comparison — almost always use `.equals()`
**String literals** share pool objects; `new String(...)` always creates a new heap object
**Compile-time concatenation** (`"a" + "b"`) is interned; **runtime concatenation** (variable + anything) creates new heap object
**substring(i, j)**: i is inclusive, j is exclusive; result has `j - i` characters; `substring(i, i)` returns empty string
**charAt(i)**: 0-based index; throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` at runtime if `i < 0` or `i >= length()`
**StringBuilder is mutable**: methods like `append()`, `delete()`, `reverse()` modify the SAME object in place
**String loop performance**: prefer `StringBuilder.append()` in loops over repeated `+` concatenation (avoids O(n²) copies)
**Conversions**: `Integer.parseInt(s)` for String→int; `String.valueOf(n)` for int→String; `toCharArray()` for String→char[]
**isEmpty()** checks length==0; **isBlank()** (Java 11+) also returns true for whitespace-only strings
**StringBuffer** = thread-safe StringBuilder; in single-threaded code, always use **StringBuilder** (faster)
